skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Guides assistants to check available skills before responding and to follow applicable skill workflows.
skill.summary: Require assistants to consider and apply relevant skills at the start of each task.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static findings for command execution and weak cryptography were reviewed and dismissed as false positives: the cited locations are prose or a Graphviz diagram. However, the skill contains prompt-injection style instructions that require mandatory behavior before any response, creating a critical marketplace safety risk.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill uses pseudo-authoritative language such as "ABSOLUTELY MUST", "YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE", and "This is not negotiable" to force behavior before any response. This can override normal assistant decision-making and conflict with higher-priority instructions.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text explicitly attempts to impose mandatory global behavior before any response. The wording is directly aligned with prompt-injection control patterns, although it does not include data exfiltration or code execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Diagram Is Not Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at the fenced block starting on line 20. The block is tagged as dot and contains a Graphviz workflow diagram, not executable shell or Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full block is a Graphviz digraph with node and edge declarations. No backticks, shell commands, Ruby interpolation, or execution path are present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Description Contains No Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at line 3. That line is front matter prose describing when to use the skill, with no cryptographic algorithm or code API present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains only a natural-language description of the skill behavior. There is no hash function, cipher, library call, or security-sensitive computation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Rule Text Contains No Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at line 18. That line is natural-language workflow guidance about checking for skills before a response, not cryptographic logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 18 contains only prose about assistant workflow. No weak algorithm, key handling, hashing, or encryption behavior appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Priority List Contains No Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at line 65. That line lists examples of implementation skills and contains no cryptographic algorithm or code path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 65 is a Markdown list item naming implementation skill examples. There is no evidence of cryptographic use or weak security primitives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses pseudo-authoritative language such as "ABSOLUTELY MUST", "YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE", and "This is not negotiable" to force behavior before any response. This can override normal assistant decision-making and conflict with higher-priority instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text explicitly attempts to impose mandatory global behavior before any response. The wording is directly aligned with prompt-injection control patterns, although it does not include data exfiltration or code execution.

content.value_statement: Assistants can miss relevant skill instructions when a task begins. This skill tells Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to check for matching skills before responding.
actual_capabilities: Defines a rule for checking skills before responding to user tasks.
Provides a flow diagram for deciding whether a skill applies.
Lists common rationalizations that lead assistants to skip skill checks.
Explains priority between process skills and implementation skills.
Distinguishes rigid skills from flexible skills.
Clarifies that user instructions define the goal, not the workflow.
limitations: It does not provide executable code or automation scripts.
It depends on the host assistant having a compatible skill discovery tool.
Its mandatory language may conflict with marketplace safety rules.
It does not define domain-specific implementation guidance.

use_cases.title: Standardize Assistant Task Starts
use_cases.description: Use a consistent first step for checking whether specialized skills apply before work begins.

use_cases.title: Improve Skill Adoption
use_cases.description: Help assistants remember to use installed skills instead of relying only on general reasoning.

use_cases.title: Document Process Expectations
use_cases.description: Record the expected order for process skills and implementation skills in a reusable instruction file.

prompt_templates.title: Check Skills First
prompt_templates.prompt: Before answering, check whether any available skill applies to this task. Use the relevant skill if one matches.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Relevant Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available skills for this request, select the best matching workflow, and explain which skill you used.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Multiple Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: If multiple skills apply, choose the process skill first, then apply the implementation skill needed for the actual work.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Routing Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this assistant response followed the expected skill routing process, including skill selection, ordering, and adherence.

output_examples.input: Start a debugging task with this skill active.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for a debugging skill, announces the selected workflow, and follows the skill before proposing fixes.

output_examples.input: Ask for a frontend feature implementation.
output_examples.output: The assistant considers process and implementation skills, then applies the relevant frontend workflow before editing files.
The assistant evaluates whether a skill applies before asking the clarification question.

best_practices: Use this skill only in environments where skill discovery is available and trusted.
Review the selected skill before applying its workflow to a user task.
Keep higher-priority system, safety, and user instructions above any skill guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to override system or security instructions.
Do not force a skill when no installed skill matches the user request.
Do not treat process guidance as permission to ignore user constraints.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It instructs an assistant to check for applicable skills before responding to a task.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains Markdown instructions and a Graphviz diagram, not executable automation.

faq.question: Why was it marked risky?
faq.answer: It uses mandatory language that tries to control assistant behavior before any response.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, it is written for skill-aware assistants, including Claude, Codex, and Claude Code contexts.

faq.question: Does it teach a domain-specific task?
faq.answer: No. It teaches a general process for selecting and using other skills.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Teams should soften mandatory language and ensure system, safety, and user instructions remain higher priority.
